strategic_theory.description: Abstract social principles explaining WHY certain strategies work

fields.principle: The core social principle or theory (1-2 sentences)
fields.explanation: Detailed explanation of why this principle works (2-4 sentences)
fields.application: How to apply this principle in real interactions (1-2 sentences)

example.principle: Active listening requires suspending judgment and focusing entirely on the speaker's perspective.
example.explanation: When people feel truly heard, their defensive barriers lower, creating psychological safety. This activates cooperative brain circuits rather than threat-response mechanisms, enabling genuine dialogue.
example.application: Practice by paraphrasing what the other person said before responding, and ask clarifying questions rather than immediately offering solutions.

illustrative_case.description: Concrete examples showing HOW social principles work in practice

fields.action: What the person did or said (1-2 sentences)
fields.outcome: What happened as a result (1-2 sentences)

example.context: A manager noticed team morale dropping after a failed product launch.
example.action: Instead of assigning blame, she held a retrospective where she first acknowledged her own mistakes, then invited each team member to share their perspective.
example.outcome: Team members felt safe to discuss real issues. They identified process gaps and rebuilt trust, leading to a successful relaunch.
example.lesson: Vulnerability from leaders creates psychological safety that enables honest team communication.

practical_scenario.description: Immersive role-play scenarios for hands-on practice of social skills

fields.title: Brief scenario title
fields.characters: List of characters with brief descriptions
fields.challenge: The core social difficulty to navigate

example.title: The Budget Meeting Standoff
example.background: You are a project manager in a quarterly budget review. Your team needs additional funding for a critical feature, but another department head is competing for the same budget allocation.

characters.name: You
characters.role: Project Manager advocating for your team

characters.name: Sarah
characters.role: Marketing Director competing for the same budget

characters.name: David
characters.role: CFO who will make the final decision

example.objective: Negotiate a fair budget allocation that addresses both teams' core needs while maintaining a collaborative relationship with Sarah.
example.challenge: Balancing assertiveness for your team's needs with empathy for Sarah's legitimate concerns, while presenting a compelling case to David.

values: Standing up for the rights of others
Reflecting on one's role to promote well-being
